subject: Chemistry
title: ClF₃: The Chemical That Sets Fire to Anything
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on ClF₃: The Chemical That Sets Fire to Anything.

hook.text: Chlorine trifluoride is so reactive it sets fire to glass, sand, asbestos, and even water. It burned through a concrete floor.

buildup.text: ClF₃ is a stronger oxidizer than oxygen itself. It rips electrons from nearly any material it contacts.

discovery.text: In one spill, a ton of ClF₃ burned through 30 cm of concrete and 90 cm of gravel beneath it before stopping.

twist.text: The Nazis tried to use ClF₃ as a weapon called 'N-stoff.' Even they found it too dangerous to handle.

climax.text: Rocket scientist John Clark wrote: 'It can be extinguished with nothing.' No firefighting method works on ClF₃ fires.

punchline.text: It burns concrete, ignites water, and has no extinguisher. Chemistry's angriest molecule.

quiz.question: What makes chlorine trifluoride so extremely dangerous?
options: It's a stronger oxidizer than oxygen and reacts violently with almost any material
It produces invisible toxic fumes that are undetectable
It explodes on contact with any metal surface
